plan.contextChunk:
$\boldsymbol{l}_4$, we can get another vanishing point by computing
their cross-product. Finally, the horizon line is the line that passes
through both points that can be computed as the cross-product of the
vanishing points in homogeneous coordinates.

![Using parallel lines, in homogeneous coordinates, to compute vanishing points and the horizon line.](figures/imaging_geometry/horizon_line.png){width="90%" #fig-horizon_line_cross_products}

### Detecting Vanishing Points

@fig-vanishing_lines shows the office scene and three vanishing points
computed manually. In this scene there are three dominant orientations
along orthogonal directions. The three vanishing points are estimated by
selecting pairs of long edges in the image corresponding to parallel 3D
lines in the scene and finding their intersection.

![Most object boundaries in this photograph are oriented parallel to one of the world-coordinates reference frame (the world-coordinates frame is chosen in this way on purpose). All the parallel 3D lines converge on the image plane in a vanishing point. In this picture there are three main vanishing points, each of them related to one of the world-coordinates frame.](figures/single_view_3d/office_vanishing_points_3.png){width="100%" #fig-vanishing_lines}

Vanishing points can be extracted automatically from images by first
detecting image edges and their orientations (like we did in chapter
@sec-simplesystem) and then using RANSAC (@sec-RANSAC) to group the edges: every pair of edges will
vote for a vanishing point and the point with the largest support will
be considered a vanishing point.

There are also learning-based approaches for vanishing point detection
using graphical models @coughlan1999manhattan, or deep learning based
approaches such as DeepVP @Chang2018DeepVPDL and NeurVPS
@zhou2019neurvps.

## Measuring Heights Using Parallel Lines

Let's now use what we have seen up to now to measurement distances in

elements: stylized 3D office room mockup with a bookshelf, a desk, and a round table
world coordinate axes frame (Xw in blue, Yw in red, Zw in green) positioned at the center of the office floor
virtual perspective camera with visible frustum, camera body, and a semi-transparent image plane
set of parallel lines along X-axis (blue edges of desk and bookshelf top)
set of parallel lines along Y-axis (red vertical edges of bookshelf and door frame)
set of parallel lines along Z-axis (green horizontal edges of bookshelf depth and floor tiles)
extended dashed projection rays from 3D scene edges onto the image plane
projected 2D lines on the camera image plane colored blue, red, and green
vanishing point v1 (blue sphere) on the projection plane, where horizontal Z-lines converge
vanishing point v2 (green sphere) on the projection plane, where horizontal X-lines converge
vanishing point v3 (red sphere) on the projection plane, where vertical Y-lines converge
horizon line (thin white line) connecting vanishing points v1 and v2

interactions.label: View Mode
interactions.teaches: Switches between a 3D perspective view showing the camera in the room, and the Camera's Eye View looking directly at the 2D projected image plane

interactions.label: Camera Tilt (Pitch)
interactions.teaches: Tilting the camera up or down shifts the vertical vanishing point v3 vertically and moves the horizon line up or down on the image plane

interactions.label: Camera Pan (Yaw)
interactions.teaches: Panning the camera left or right shifts the vanishing points v1 and v2 horizontally along the horizon line

interactions.label: Show Vanishing Rays
interactions.teaches: Extends the projected 2D edges past the physical object boundaries to show exactly how they converge at the vanishing points v1, v2, and v3

demo_steps.title: 3D Scene & Orthogonal Directions
demo_steps.narration: Welcome to the 3D office scene. Notice how the room's geometry aligns with three orthogonal axes: X (blue), Y (red), and Z (green). The camera in the corner is pointed at the scene, projecting these 3D structures onto its 2D image plane.

demo_steps.title: Camera View: Finding Convergence
demo_steps.narration: Let's switch to the camera's point of view and enable vanishing rays. Notice how parallel 3D lines are no longer parallel in the 2D projection! The green depth edges converge at vanishing point v1 on the left, and the blue horizontal edges converge at v2 on the right.

demo_steps.title: The Horizon Line
demo_steps.narration: The line connecting v1 and v2 is the horizon line. Because both the X and Z axes lie on the horizontal ground plane, their vanishing points must lie on this line. Any other set of parallel horizontal lines in this room would also converge somewhere on this same horizon.

demo_steps.title: The Vertical Vanishing Point v3
demo_steps.narration: Look downward: the red vertical lines (Y-axis) are not parallel either—they converge at vanishing point v3 far below. Since the camera is tilted slightly downward (pitch = -10°), these vertical lines tilt inward as they go down.

demo_steps.title: Leveling the Camera
demo_steps.narration: If we level the camera to a horizontal pitch of 0°, look at what happens: the vertical lines on the wall and bookshelf become perfectly vertical on screen, and the vertical vanishing point v3 moves to infinity, disappearing from the frame.

demo_steps.title: Panning the Horizon
demo_steps.narration: Finally, pan the camera to the right (Yaw = 15°). Watch how the vanishing points v1 and v2 slide along the horizon line. The physical directions in the room stay constant, but their projections shift dynamically across the image sensor as the camera rotates.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Elevated 3D perspective view from the opposite corner of the room, looking down at both the physical office furniture and the virtual camera setup to clearly show the relationship between 3D space and 2D projection
interactionPlan.notes: When 'viewMode' is 1, smoothly animate the Three.js camera to match the position and orientation of the virtual camera. When 'viewMode' is 0, pull the camera back to a third-person view. Calculate the projected vanishing points in screen space by projecting 3D direction vectors [1,0,0], [0,1,0], [0,0,1] to the camera's projection plane, and draw the dashed extending lines from the edge segments to these points.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The background of the original image is a photorealistic office scene, whereas the generated figure uses simplified, stylized 3D shapes (low-poly bookshelf, desk, and table).
The bookshelf in the original image is dark brown/black with books, while the generated version is light brown with colorful blocks.
The door/window frame in the background of the original is metallic/white, while in the generated model it is a simple brown wooden door frame.
evaluation.notes: Excellent 3D interactive recreation of a 2D perspective projection diagram, allowing users to dynamically tilt/pan the camera and watch the vanishing points and horizon line adjust in real time.
action_items: Add more realistic texture colors to the bookshelf and walls to closer match the original photograph's aesthetic.
Refine the 'Camera's eye view' mode to precisely match the framing of the original textbook photo when activated.
Slightly adjust the spacing of the v1/v2/v3 labels to prevent any potential overlap with the projection lines when the camera is tilted.

feedback.rationale: With an overall score of 4.8 and perfect scores in geometry, interactivity, labels, and concept, the interactive diagram is highly successful; the minor visual style differences with the original photo do not warrant further iteration.
action_items: Add more realistic texture colors to the bookshelf and walls to closer match the original photograph's aesthetic.
Refine the 'Camera's eye view' mode to precisely match the framing of the original textbook photo when activated.
Slightly adjust the spacing of the v1/v2/v3 labels to prevent any potential overlap with the projection lines when the camera is tilted.
